picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet's Q1 2026 revenue hit $109.9B (+20% YoY, fastest growth since 2022) with Google Cloud up 63% and a $460B backlog, delivering a 10% single-day stock surge post-earnings. The cloud monetization thesis is freshly validated and analyst revisions are still catching up to actual results, making this a rare large-cap where the fundamental print has outrun the price re-rating. Ackman holds it at ~12.5% of Pershing Square and AI infrastructure spend from hyperscalers directly supports Search and Cloud revenue durability in the current late-cycle regime.
picks.catalyst: Google Cloud continued monetization ramp and potential Gemini AI search monetization disclosure at Q2 2026 earnings
picks.entryNote: Trading near post-earnings levels; consensus forward P/E ~22x on accelerating EPS revisions — meaningfully cheaper than 2021 comps at identical growth rates; consolidating above 50dma post the +10% gap
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY OR Search ad revenue shows structural share loss to AI-native competitors
picks.keyRisk: AI-native search disruption (Perplexity, ChatGPT) eroding core Search monetization faster than Cloud offsets

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO has rallied ~45% since late March and today trades at $424 (intraday high $438.90), with next earnings June 3 still representing a 'buy the revisions' window as both Google and Meta — Broadcom's primary custom silicon partners — beat and raised, locking in forward XPU/networking revenue visibility. The Zacks consensus projects FY2027 sales up 40.5% and earnings up 39.5%, underpinned by hyperscaler custom chip commitments that are contractually multi-year. This is the highest-quality AI infrastructure pick in the book: 60%+ gross margins, best-in-class FCF yield, and a diversified semiconductor + infrastructure software model that reduces single-customer concentration risk versus pure-play AI chip names.
picks.catalyst: June 3, 2026 fiscal Q2 earnings report with guidance update reflecting META and GOOGL custom chip ramps
picks.entryNote: 52-week high today at $438.90, current ~$424 — technically at the top of the breakout range. P/E stretched on trailing basis but forward FCF yield remains attractive vs. peers. Entry here is momentum-driven; consider scaling in given technical extension.
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a major hyperscaler design win OR gross margin falls below 58% non-GAAP indicating pricing pressure
picks.keyRisk: GOOGL-Marvell custom chip collaboration (announced April 14) signals potential partial displacement of AVGO in Google's XPU roadmap

picks.name: Microsoft Corporation
picks.rationale: Microsoft's Q3 FY2026 print — $82.9B revenue (+18% YoY), EPS $4.27 (+21% YoY), Azure/cloud +29% YoY — came in above consensus across the board, yet the stock sold off to $413-420 as the market fretted about $190B in 2026 capex and gross margin dipping to 68%. This post-earnings weakness is a textbook setup: the stock hit a 52-week low of $356.28 in late March and has recovered to ~$418, still 25% below the $555 ATH, trading at its 'cheapest forward P/E since 2023' per analyst commentary. Azure AI monetization — Copilot in general availability, Agent 365 now live — is early-stage revenue that will compound through FY2027 guidance of $19.09 EPS, and Berkshire's Ackman holds AMZN and META as proxies for the same cloud-AI trade.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings on July 28, 2026 with Azure acceleration data and Copilot seat monetization update
picks.entryNote: ~$418, ~25% below 52-week high; forward P/E approximately 26x on FY2027 consensus — cheapest since 2023 for a business growing cloud 29% YoY; EBITDA margin 57.7%; trading in mid-range of $405-$428 intraday band
picks.exitTrigger: Azure growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters OR capex ramp destroys FCF below $50B annually
picks.keyRisk: Gross margin structural compression if AI infrastructure costs outpace revenue monetization of Copilot/Azure AI

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman's Q1 2026 earnings validated the capital markets cycle thesis: strong trading revenue, robust M&A pipeline, and NIM expansion as the yield curve steepens to +49bps on 2s10s. In the current late-cycle/stagflation-adjacent regime, GS benefits from volatility in rates and equities (FICC trading), a reopening M&A calendar as deal-certainty returns post-Iran ceasefire, and insurance-like asset management fee growth via alternatives. The sector scorecard identifies financials as 'coiling' with improving 5-day relative strength, and Druckenmiller's largest Q4 buy was XLF — a strong macro validation of the financial sector rotation thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected mid-July) with M&A pipeline disclosure and FICC trading revenue amid Warsh Fed transition volatility
picks.entryNote: Trading near YTD highs; forward P/E ~12x — discount to historical 13-14x on higher earnings power; steepening curve is a structural NIM tailwind; technically above 50dma and 200dma
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS spreads widen above 500bps (credit cycle turn) OR yield curve re-inverts, collapsing NIM expansion thesis
picks.keyRisk: Credit quality deterioration in leveraged lending if stagflation deepens into a hard landing scenario

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY reported Q1 2026 revenue of $19.8B with non-GAAP EPS of $8.55 beating the $6.73 consensus, raised full-year guidance to $82-85B revenue and $35.50-$37.00 EPS, and the cardiometabolic franchise (Mounjaro/Zepbound) generated $12.8B globally — a $6.7B YoY increase. At ~$935 and 27x forward earnings, LLY is the rare 'super-defensive growth' name: GLP-1 demand is recession-resistant, domestic manufacturing investment of $27B provides supply-chain insulation against tariff shocks, and the pipeline extends into Alzheimer's (donanemab) and oncology. This is a thesis carry-over with intact fundamentals and macro tailwinds from a stagflation-adjacent regime where defensive + secular growth commands a premium.
picks.catalyst: Zepbound obesity label expansion and Phase 3 donanemab Alzheimer's data readout; Q2 2026 earnings in late July
picks.entryNote: ~$935, 27x forward FY2026 EPS — reasonable for 56% YoY revenue growth; pulled back from prior highs offering a better entry than April; well above 200dma confirming uptrend
picks.exitTrigger: GLP-1 competitive pressure from NVO's CagriSema drives material market share loss OR surprise Medicare drug price negotiation covers Mounjaro/Zepbound
picks.keyRisk: Biosimilar/competitive GLP-1 entrants from NVO, Roche, or Pfizer compressing ASP faster than volume growth offsets

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: The sector scorecard ranks Energy #1 with XLE +29% YTD and confirms Iran war premium sustaining upstream FCF yields of 8-12% for integrated majors. XOM is the largest US integrated major with Permian Basin low-cost production, Pioneer acquisition synergies still being realized, and a structural $15B+ annual buyback program. Today XOM fell 4% on oil price softness (per Yahoo Finance headline), creating a tactical re-entry opportunity against the structural geopolitical backdrop — Goldman warns Brent could average $100+ through 2026 if the Strait remains restricted. FCF yield at $94/bbl oil is compelling; dividend growth streak intact through energy cycles. Berkshire holds OXY as its energy expression; XOM is the lower-risk, higher-liquidity pair.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Pioneer integration synergy update and FCF realization at current oil prices
picks.entryNote: Pulled back ~4% today on oil price softness — tactical entry against structural trend; 52-week high set in Q1; FCF yield ~8-9% at $94 oil; trades at ~13x forward earnings, below 10-year median
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude falls below $70/bbl on sustained basis OR Strait of Hormuz fully reopens eliminating geopolitical premium
picks.keyRisk: Ceasefire/peace deal in Middle East deflating the Iran war oil premium that underpins the energy sector #1 ranking

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX sits at the intersection of the two secular themes confirmed by the sector scorecard: defense spending (Iran war driving NATO/US DoD budget expansion) and commercial aerospace (travel demand recovery driving Pratt & Whitney engine deliveries). The sector scorecard ranks Industrials #2 YTD (+12%), with defense/aerospace/electrification as the primary sub-themes. RTX's Q1 2026 bookings visibility, Patriot missile system demand from Europe, and Pratt GTF engine ramp provide multi-year revenue locked in at backlog levels. Investors are 'aggressively rotating into the physical security playbooks of Energy and Aerospace & Defense' per the May 7 scorecard update — RTX is the highest-quality expression of that thesis. This is a thesis carry-over from the May 6 book.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with defense bookings update and Pratt GTF powder metal remediation cost trajectory
picks.entryNote: ~$130-135 range; forward P/E ~21x on multi-year backlog; trading at a premium to Lockheed but justified by commercial aerospace optionality; above 50dma confirming uptrend post-Iran war escalation
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire + broader Middle East de-escalation eliminates the geopolitical defense premium OR Pratt GTF remediation costs exceed guidance materially
picks.keyRisk: Pratt & Whitney GTF powder metal engine remediation scope expands, creating multi-billion surprise charges and airframer relationship risk

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: Vistra is the highest-quality AI power play in the universe with an 81% trailing ROE, 7.2% forward FCF yield, ~18x forward P/E, and 10x forward EV/EBITDA — the cheapest valuation among power companies benefiting from data center electricity demand. Q1 2026 earnings report today (May 7) is the single most important near-term catalyst: consensus pegged revenues at $5.4B (+38.5% YoY). An investment grade upgrade from Fitch already de-risks the balance sheet. The sector scorecard confirms Utilities as a relevant late-cycle + AI power theme. This is a thesis carry-over with 50%+ median analyst upside implied at prior target prices.
picks.catalyst: Q1 2026 earnings release today (May 7) with power purchase agreement and data center contract update
picks.entryNote: Pulled back from prior highs; ~$155 with median analyst target implying 50%+ upside; forward FCF yield 7.2% is best-in-class among AI power plays; hold above $140 support
picks.exitTrigger: Q1 earnings miss guidance materially OR a regulatory adverse ruling on nuclear plant capacity auction pricing in PJM
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory/FERC adverse ruling on capacity pricing that curtails Vistra's nuclear plant revenue expectations

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a 'toll-road' on financial information and ratings — every bond issuance, every structured product, every data subscription flows through its ecosystem. In the current regime with HY OAS widening from 264 to 328bps, refinancing activity and covenant surveillance both drive ratings agency volumes. The Commodity Insights division benefits directly from the energy shock and Iran war commodity volatility. SPGI's market data franchise (Platts, IHS Markit integrations) compounds at 10-12% organically, with 70%+ recurring revenue providing defensive characteristics within the financials book. This is a diversifying pick within Financials that is distinct from GS's capital markets cyclicality.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with issuance volume data and Market Intelligence subscription growth disclosure
picks.entryNote: Forward P/E ~28x — premium to market but justified by 70%+ recurring revenue, 35%+ margins, and ROIC >20%; near 52-week highs reflecting financial information demand
picks.exitTrigger: Bond issuance volumes collapse below 2020 levels on credit freeze OR DOJ reopens antitrust scrutiny of ratings oligopoly
picks.keyRisk: Debt issuance cycle turns sharply negative if stagflation deepens into credit freeze, collapsing the core ratings revenue stream

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AWS Q1 2026 cloud revenue growth accelerated to +28% (from +24% in Q4 2025 and +20% in Q3 2025), and Amazon disclosed $225B in Trainium chip revenue commitments — the largest single AI hardware backlog of any hyperscaler. Ackman increased AMZN by 65% to make it his second-largest position at 14.3% of Pershing Square. The monetization narrative has been 'satisfactorily answered' per institutional analysis, and AWS margin expansion remains the primary EPS driver for FY2026-2027. As a Consumer Discretionary name by GICS, this adds sector diversification to the growth book while the core thesis is a cloud/AI infrastructure growth story identical in structure to GOOGL and MSFT.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with AWS revenue acceleration curve and Trainium revenue ramp disclosure
picks.entryNote: Post-earnings consolidation phase; still well below prior ATH levels; AWS acceleration curve and $225B Trainium backlog not yet fully reflected in Street estimates; Ackman's 65% position increase at current levels is a strong conviction signal
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters OR retail operating margin contracts below 5% on tariff/logistics headwinds
picks.keyRisk: Retail segment margin compression from tariff pass-through costs and logistics inflation squeezing the non-AWS businesses that mask the cloud story

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold crossed $5,300/oz per sector scorecard May 7 data, confirming the secular safe-haven bid driven by Iran war geopolitical premium, USD debasement risk from record fiscal deficits, and real negative policy rates (funds rate 3.625% vs core PCE 3.2%). In a stagflation-adjacent regime where the equity risk premium is negative (earnings yield 3.82% vs 10y at 4.38%), GLD provides zero-beta ballast with positive carry in real terms versus cash. Berkshire's record $370B+ cash hoard signals macro caution consistent with GLD's risk-off role. This is the portfolio's primary inflation hedge and tail-risk insurance.
picks.catalyst: Iran war escalation or ceasefire breakdown pushing oil above $100/bbl, triggering stagflation premium re-pricing
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; next acute window is the May 12 CPI print
picks.entryNote: Gold near $5,300; technically extended but in a confirmed uptrend; entry appropriate given portfolio insurance function rather than return-maximization; scale in on pullbacks toward $5,100
picks.exitTrigger: Credible Iran ceasefire + Fed pivot to cuts simultaneously eliminates both geopolitical premium and stagflation hedge value
picks.keyRisk: A surprise ceasefire deal + soft CPI print could trigger a sharp -10% to -15% reversal in gold as geopolitical and inflation premiums deflate simultaneously

picks.name: Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is up 12% in the past six months with a 3.3% SEC yield at 0.06% expense ratio, screening for 10+ years of consecutive dividends and ranking on FCF/debt, ROE, yield, and 5-year dividend growth. With only 9% tech exposure, SCHD provides genuine diversification away from the AI concentration risk embedded in the benchmark — critical in a portfolio that already has GOOGL, AVGO, MSFT in the growth book. In a late-cycle regime with the equity risk premium at near-zero, SCHD's quality income sleeve is the rational 'parking lot' for capital that should be earning more than Treasuries while carrying lower duration risk than TLT. Druckenmiller's large RSP purchase is directionally consistent with this 'market breadth' positioning.
picks.catalyst: Market rotation into dividend quality accelerates as growth names face multiple compression from persistently high rates
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; accelerates if 10-year yield breaks above 4.6% forcing re-allocation from growth to income
picks.entryNote: Up 12% over 6 months but still at reasonable valuation relative to constituent earnings; 3.3% yield provides floor support; low-beta behavior confirmed in Q1 2026 drawdown
picks.exitTrigger: Fed delivers emergency rate cuts (sub-2%) creating a yield-chasing environment where SCHD's income advantage disappears against falling-rate growth alternatives
picks.keyRisk: A sharp Fed pivot to aggressive easing would make SCHD's dividend yield less competitive versus re-rating growth names

picks.name: Linde plc
picks.rationale: Linde is the world's largest industrial gas company — a pricing-power compounder with take-or-pay contracts, 60%+ recurring revenue, and direct exposure to the hydrogen economy, chip manufacturing (high-purity gases for semiconductor fabs), and industrial decarbonization. In the sector scorecard, Materials ranks #3 YTD (+17%) driven by AI infrastructure and reshoring demand, with Linde being the highest-quality, lowest-volatility expression of that theme. As a defensive-growth hybrid with ~25x forward earnings and 15%+ ROIC, LIN provides downside protection via contract structure while participating in the secular industrial capex supercycle. It replaces IEF from the prior day's defensive sleeve given the stagflation regime makes long-duration Treasuries structurally unattractive.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with clean energy project backlog update and semiconductor gas volume growth
picks.entryNote: Near 52-week highs; 25x forward earnings is within historical range for this quality compounder; take-or-pay contracts provide earnings floor; technically above all moving averages
picks.exitTrigger: Global industrial production contracts sharply reducing take-or-pay gas volumes OR hydrogen economy investment cycle reverses on policy change
picks.keyRisk: Global manufacturing recession reduces industrial gas volumes beyond what take-or-pay minimums can protect against

picks.name: AbbVie Inc.
picks.rationale: ABBV surged 19% in the 2022 bear market, demonstrating crisis-tested defensive characteristics from its deep immunology and oncology portfolio. The Humira patent cliff has been successfully cleared with Skyrizi and Rinvoq now fully absorbing lost revenue, and the dividend — yielding approximately 3.5% with a multi-decade growth track record — provides income durability in a high-rate environment. In the current late-cycle stagflation-adjacent regime, essential chronic disease medications exhibit inelastic demand. ABBV provides healthcare sector exposure complementary to LLY's growth character in the growth book, diversifying the pharma thesis across pricing archetypes: blockbuster GLP-1 (LLY) versus immunology/oncology royalty-like revenue streams (ABBV).
picks.catalyst: Skyrizi and Rinvoq combined quarterly revenue run-rate exceeding $8B, confirming Humira cliff has been fully offset
picks.entryNote: ~3.5% dividend yield provides income floor; low beta (0.4-0.5); defensive pharma premium justified in late-cycle; trades at ~17x forward earnings — reasonable for a compounder with 70%+ recurring formulary revenue
picks.exitTrigger: Skyrizi or Rinvoq face unexpected clinical setbacks, safety label changes, or biosimilar competition earlier than expected
picks.keyRisk: Unexpected adverse Phase 3 data for a pipeline candidate or surprise Medicare drug pricing negotiation targeting immunology drugs

picks.name: Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO operates in 200+ countries, has raised its dividend for 60+ consecutive years, generates substantial FCF from an asset-light concentrate model, and trades at ~22x forward earnings — not cheap in absolute terms but providing 0.5-0.6 beta behavior with genuine income in a negative equity risk premium environment. Consumer Staples rank #10 on the scorecard and are up ~15% YTD, but KO specifically benefits from its global brand moat and pricing power even against middle-income consumer pressure from energy inflation. Berkshire holds KO at ~10.2% of the book, unchanged — a 60-year conviction signal from the world's most scrutinized value investor. In the stagflation-adjacent regime, consumers cut discretionary before staples.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with organic revenue growth and pricing power data across EM markets where FX tailwinds are building
picks.entryNote: ~$72-75 range; 22x forward earnings; 3%+ dividend yield; 52-week range confirms range-bound defensive stability; Berkshire's unchanged position signals conviction floor
picks.exitTrigger: Volume declines accelerate globally as energy-cost squeeze forces even staples trade-down to private label, OR dividend growth halts
picks.keyRisk: USD strength from safe-haven demand compresses international revenue translation, masking underlying organic growth strength

summary: The portfolio expresses a late-cycle, stagflation-adjacent regime thesis where the Fed is pinned at 3.5-3.75% with core PCE at 3.2%, the equity risk premium is near zero (earnings yield 3.82% vs 10y at 4.38%), and capital is rotating toward quality, pricing power, and real assets. The growth book concentrates on two structural secular themes — AI infrastructure (GOOGL, AVGO, MSFT, AMZN) and inflation/cycle beneficiaries (XOM, RTX, VST, GS, SPGI) — with LLY as the 'super-defensive growth' anchor that straddles both sleeves in character. Key carry-overs from prior days include GOOGL (#1, thesis intact post-earnings validation), AVGO (#2, June 3 earnings catalyst approaching), GS (#3 carry, Druckenmiller XLF macro backing), LLY (#4, raised guidance intact), and AMZN (re-added from May 6 book with Ackman's 65% position increase as conviction support); XOM replaces pure-energy ETF exposure with a name-level entry on today's 4% pullback. The dominant risk being underwritten is AI capex ROI disappointment — if hyperscalers signal a pause in data center spend, GOOGL, AVGO, MSFT, and AMZN reprice simultaneously, which is partially offset by the defensive sleeve's gold, dividend-income, and low-beta pharma/staples positioning.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, MSFT), Health Care (LLY), Financials (GS, SPGI), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), Utilities (VST), and Consumer Discretionary (AMZN) — no sector exceeds 3 names; the AI infrastructure theme is expressed across GOOGL+AVGO+MSFT+AMZN but at different value-chain points (search/cloud, custom silicon, enterprise software, cloud infrastructure) reducing co-movement risk. The defensive sleeve spans 4 distinct exposures: gold (GLD), broad dividend quality income (SCHD), materials/industrial compounder (LIN), pharma defensive (ABBV), and consumer staples (KO) — deliberately omitting long-duration Treasuries (TLT/IEF) given the stagflation-adjacent regime makes duration unattractive.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc. Class A
picks.rationale: Alphabet delivered a 24/25 momentum score on the multi-factor screen: Google Cloud revenue surged 63% YoY to $20.03B — faster than Azure and AWS — while Search revenue grew 19% YoY to $60.4B, obliterating the AI-disruption bear case. At ~29x trailing P/E (52-week range $147–$387), the stock is at the top of its range but the earnings trajectory justifies it: EPS of $5.11 crushed the $2.63 consensus estimate, and analyst EPS estimates were revised up 5.2% post-print. Ackman's Pershing Square holds GOOGL at ~12.5% of portfolio (post-rebalance), corroborating institutional conviction. In a late-cycle/stagflationary regime where the equity risk premium is negative, GOOGL's cloud monetization and $180–$190B AI capex commitment to infrastructure creates a durable moat that compounds through the cycle.
picks.catalyst: April CPI print (due May 12) plus GOOGL's Google Cloud Next conference pipeline announcements and any Azure/AWS share-shift data in Q2 cloud earnings
picks.entryNote: GOOGL trading at ~$385–$386, right at its 52-week high after a blowout Q1 print; trailing P/E ~29x is premium but significantly below its AI-cycle peer set (AVGO at 82x, NVDA at >100x TTM); consolidating at the breakout level — add on any 3–5% pullback toward $365 for better asymmetry
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or DOJ antitrust ruling structurally impairs Search monetization
picks.keyRisk: $180–$190B AI capex guidance raised for 2026 compresses near-term FCF; if Cloud growth disappoints even one quarter at this valuation, the stock re-rates -15 to -20%

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom is the single most compelling AI infrastructure compounder in the semiconductor space with differentiated exposure: AI semiconductor revenue hit $8.4B (+106% YoY) in Q1 2026 with Q2 guidance projecting ~140% YoY growth, and the CEO has targeted $100B in AI chip revenue by 2027. Unlike NVDA (which faces export control and custom silicon competition risk), AVGO's XPU business spans 6 hyperscaler customers with multi-year supply secured through 2028, giving exceptional earnings visibility. Gross margin ~77%, FCF of $8B (41% of revenue), and operating margin ~66% constitute the highest quality overlay in the semiconductor universe. The stock is up ~127% over the past year but is being added to by institutional holders (Alecta Pensionsforsakring boosted its stake by 398,900 shares in late April 2026), confirming accumulation at current levels.
picks.catalyst: Broadcom Q2 FY2026 earnings report (expected early June 2026) with XPU revenue run-rate update and FY2027 AI chip revenue guidance
picks.entryNote: AVGO trading at ~$415–$423 (52-week range $175–$423); P/E ratio ~82x TTM is elevated vs. history but justified by 140% YoY AI revenue growth trajectory; stock at all-time highs — size at 23% and do not chase; target entry on consolidation near $400 (prior breakout zone)
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of any single major hyperscaler XPU design win, or non-AI semiconductor revenue deterioration accelerates beyond -15% YoY for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Heavy customer concentration — if any one of the 6 XPU hyperscaler relationships shifts to in-house silicon (as Alphabet has begun exploring Marvell alternatives), the revenue cliff is steep and swift

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the mandatory late-cycle/stagflationary hedge in this portfolio. Gold is trading at ~$4,621/oz as of May 4, 2026, with J.P. Morgan projecting prices toward $5,000/oz by Q4 2026, and Wells Fargo targeting $6,100–$6,300 by year-end. The macro backdrop is near-perfect for gold: the Iran/Strait of Hormuz conflict is unresolved with the US maintaining its naval blockade, the DXY has weakened to ~98.60, fiscal deficits are running at 5.9% of GDP (expanding toward 7–7.5%), and the equity risk premium has flipped negative. GLD has returned 8.47% YTD vs SPY's 5.16%, and central banks bought 244 tonnes in Q1 2026 alone (+3% YoY), with the World Gold Council affirming that geopolitics and investment demand will remain the primary support structure. This is the portfolio's tail-risk buffer against both a stagflation trap (scenario 1) and a Treasury market stress event (scenario 2) — the only asset that benefits from both simultaneously.
picks.catalyst: Iran/Strait of Hormuz escalation (military briefing to Trump confirmed); Kevin Warsh Fed Chair confirmation (May 15) signaling hawkish pivot that pressures DXY; April CPI upside print (May 12)
picks.entryNote: GLD at ~$148–$152 per share (underlying gold ~$4,600/oz); after correcting from the January 2026 high of $5,405/oz, gold is in consolidation mid-range — the WGC notes investment demand far exceeds fabrication demand; this is a strategic, not tactical, position — sized at 20% as a portfolio hedge
picks.exitTrigger: Comprehensive Iran nuclear deal signed AND Strait of Hormuz fully reopened AND 10-year yield falls below 3.5% (eliminating stagflation premium) — the three-way resolution that removes all three tailwinds simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: A 'risk-on' relief rally driven by a surprise Iran peace deal could temporarily knock gold 10–15%, as energy prices fall and the stagflation premium deflates; sized accordingly as a hedge, not a pure return driver

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-quality name in Financials and the lowest-extended pick on the screen (score 19/25, 'Extended: Low'). The yield curve has re-steepened to +51bps (2s10s), directly expanding net interest margins and revitalizing trading revenue. Goldman's institutional positioning is ideal for this regime: record trading revenues from elevated volatility, M&A recovery as AI capex financing accelerates (SPGI's ratings revenue confirms record debt issuance), and ROE above 15% with improving ROTCE. Regional banks like SouthState and Regions are benefiting from the steepening curve, but GS captures the capital markets and trading premium that smaller banks cannot. The sector scorecard explicitly rates XLF 'Outperform for 2026, supported by robust bank profitability and resilient economic growth,' and the 2s10s steepening is the structural tailwind that compounds through the hold period. GS has beaten EPS estimates in each of the last 4 reported quarters.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) with investment banking and FICC trading revenue update; Kevin Warsh confirmation (May 15) signaling hawkish institutional tone that directly benefits fixed-income trading volumes
picks.catalystWindow: May 15 (Warsh confirmation) through mid-July earnings (next 10 weeks)
picks.entryNote: GS trading at roughly fair value on 10-year median NTM P/E (~12–13x); the financials rally has been fundamentally supported — unlike tech peers, GS is NOT extended. The steepening curve at +51bps is still in early innings vs. historical norms of +100–150bps, giving significant NIM upside. Entry here is well within historical valuation range.
picks.exitTrigger: 2s10s spread inverts back below 0bps (curve flattens), or FICC trading revenues decline >20% YoY for two consecutive quarters signaling normalization of volatility premium
picks.keyRisk: A rapid resolution of the Iran conflict could compress energy/FX volatility, reduce FICC trading revenues, and simultaneously cause the long end to rally — shrinking the NIM tailwind and hurting the steepening thesis in a single event

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY is the portfolio's defensive-growth diversifier: GLP-1 drugs (Mounjaro, Zepbound) represent a multi-decade, recession-immune demand wave with no correlation to AI capex cycles, energy prices, or yield curve dynamics. The healthcare sector scorecard flags XLV at ~19.94x forward P/E — below SPY — with 'historically large discounts' and the sector 'positioned for mean reversion and potential outperformance in 2026.' Biotech/Pharma leading names are returning to favor as 'much of the worry about tariffs and interest rates has faded.' LLY specifically benefits from the healthcare employment boom (76K jobs added in March, the strongest sectoral print), which signals sector-level capital deployment. At ~30x forward earnings vs. the 50–60x peak multiple in 2024, the risk/reward is materially improved with gross margins expanding and IP exclusivity through 2032+. This is the portfolio's only true sector-diversifier from the tech/financial/commodity axis.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 readout on orforglipron (oral GLP-1 pill) expected H2 2026 — a positive result opens a $50B+ TAM expansion beyond injectable formats and would be the single largest re-rating event for the stock
picks.entryNote: LLY is off its 2024 highs (~$800+); trading at ~30x forward earnings vs. 5-year avg of 40–50x, representing the most attractive entry point since the GLP-1 thesis became consensus. Healthcare sector outflows (-$727M 3-month net flows in XLV) create the contrarian entry dynamic the sector scorecard explicitly flagged.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro strategy briefing as of **Monday, May 4, 2026**.

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO DASHBOARD — May 4, 2026

---

### FED POLICY


The Fed held the federal funds rate steady at the **3.50%–3.75%** target range for a 2nd consecutive meeting in March 2026, in line with expectations.
 
The Fed is widely expected to keep the rate unchanged at the 3.50%–3.75% range for a third consecutive meeting in April 2026, as policymakers navigate an increasingly complex environment.


**Dot plot (March 2026):** 
The March 2026 Fed dot plot projected a **3.00%–3.25%** fed funds rate by year-end 2027.
 
Policymakers still signaled one reduction in the fed funds rate this year and another in 2027, though the timing remains unclear.
 **Market-implied path:** 
The outlook for the rest of the year remains uncertain, with oil prices rising and inflation picking up due to the energy shock — markets currently expect **no changes to rates this year**.


**Real policy rate vs. neutral:** 
With the policy rate close to neutral, the IMF cautioned that there is little room to cut interest rates in 2026, particularly given the rise in energy prices, the likely passthrough to core inflation, and upside risks to global commodity prices.
 
The Fed has cut rates by **175 bps** in total since September 2024.


---

### YIELD CURVE


As of May 1, 2026: **10y = 4.39%**, **2y = 3.88%**, **30y = 4.97%**.
 The **2s10s spread = ~+51 bps** — positively sloped, confirming curve re-steepening in progress. 
The 3m10y spread was deeply negative from October 2022 to December 2024, and since February 2026 has been swinging back and forth from positive to negative territory.
 
The 10-year yield remains sharply higher this year as inflation threats on a robust economy erased bets of a Fed cut in 2026.
 
Treasury yields have been trending higher since the onset of the Middle East conflict, driven by concerns that elevated energy prices could reignite inflation.
 **Term premium direction: rising** — the long end is bearing the brunt of energy/fiscal concerns, with the 30y at nearly 5%.

---

### INFLATION


Core PCE prices **accelerated** in March.
 
The Fed projects PCE inflation to remain elevated, with PCE inflation expected to end 2026 at **2.7%**, 30 bps higher than the December 2025 projection.
 
Inflation moved sideways during 2025 as tariffs boosted goods prices while services inflation moderated.
 
ISM Manufacturing prices paid soared to a **4-year high** in April, as companies experienced higher energy costs in the wake of the Iran war alongside elevated tariff charges.
 Trajectory: **re-accelerating** on a goods/energy basis, with supercore (core services ex-shelter) sticky due to tight labor market. 
The median FOMC participant revised inflation projections upward for 2026 and 2027, though still expects return to target by 2028.


---

### GROWTH


US consumption slowed in Q1 2026, though surging AI investment supported **+2.0% annualized GDP expansion** in Q1.
 
GDP growth is expected to accelerate modestly in 2026 to **2.4%** (Q4/Q4 basis).


**Payrolls run-rate:** 
The US economy added **178K jobs** in March 2026, the most since December 2024, coming in well above the market expectation of 60K.
 The Jan–Mar 3-month run-rate is roughly **+68K/month** (dragged by the strike-distorted Feb print of -133K). 
Over the year, average hourly earnings have increased by **3.5%**.


**ISM:** 
The April ISM Manufacturing index totaled **52.7**, unchanged from March and slightly below expectations; the prices paid component surged to its highest level since April 2022, driven by higher energy costs and tariff charges.


---

### CREDIT & LIQUIDITY


Following three hawkish dissents at the April Fed meeting, core PCE acceleration and energy-driven ISM prices signal a tightening bias that bears watching for credit spreads.
 HY OAS data from FRED's ICE BofA index is directionally widening amid the Middle East risk premium, though not yet at distressed levels. The 30y Treasury at ~4.97% and fiscal deficits 
of 5.9% of GDP in FY2025
 continue to pressure the long end. 
The general government deficit is expected to remain in the **7–7.5% of GDP** range with debt exceeding 140% of GDP by 2031.
 **DXY:** The dollar has firmed modestly on "higher for longer" repricing and geopolitical safe-haven demand.

---

### EARNINGS BACKDROP


The S&P 500 trailing earnings yield stood at **3.82%** as of May 1, 2026
, implying a **trailing P/E of ~26.2x** — materially above the 10-year average of ~19–20x. 
The forward earnings yield with estimates was **4.10%** as of year-end 2026 projections
, implying a **forward P/E of ~24.4x**. The equity risk premium (earnings yield minus 10y at 4.39%) is **negative at ~-58 bps** — stocks are priced for perfection relative to bonds, with bonds now offering a meaningful competing yield. 
The S&P 500 was heading for its best month since 2020 in April, with Apple earnings on deck
 — a relief rally following earlier volatility.

---

### GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE


Iran delivered a new proposal for the US to consider after President Trump rejected previous concessions and pledged to maintain the naval blockade, which together with Iran's threats has suspended energy and goods exports from the Middle East.
 
Crude oil remains on track for a second consecutive weekly gain amid fading hopes for a US–Iran peace deal and expectations that the Strait of Hormuz will remain closed near-term.
 
The April FOMC meeting could mark Powell's final meeting as Chair — the Justice Department said it would halt its criminal investigation into Powell, removing a key obstacle to the Senate's confirmation of his nominated successor, **Kevin Warsh**, whose appointment is set for **May 15**.


---

## 📋 REGIME BRIEFING — 3 PARAGRAPHS

---

### 1. 🔴 REGIME CALL: **Late-Cycle / Transition Zone**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones** — the economy is expanding (2% real GDP, 52.7 ISM Mfg, robust labor market), but inflation is **re-accelerating** courtesy of an energy shock from the Iran conflict and residual tariff pass-through, 
with the IMF explicitly warning that the policy rate is close to neutral, leaving little room to cut
, while 
three hawkish dissents at the April FOMC meeting signal the committee is leaning against easing.
 The curve is bear-steepening (30y at 4.97%), the equity risk premium has flipped negative, and markets have fully priced out 2026 cuts — the classic late-cycle fingerprint of a Fed trapped between sticky inflation and a resilient economy.

---

### 2. ✅ WHAT'S WORKING / ❌ WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** Energy (direct beneficiary of the Iran supply shock — oil producers, MLP infrastructure), **Defense & Aerospace** (geopolitical spending premium), **Value/Dividend equities** (higher long yields compress duration-sensitive growth, rotating capital to cash-flow-heavy names), **Healthcare** (defensive earnings, 
healthcare added 76K jobs in March, the strongest sectoral print
, signaling sector strength), and **Commodities/Materials** (inflation re-acceleration + supply disruption). **AI/Infrastructure Capex** remains supported — 
surging AI-related investment helped sustain GDP growth in Q1.


**Breaking:** Long-duration growth equities (negative ERP, 10y at 4.39% competes directly with ~4.1% forward earnings yield), 
Financial Services (financial activities employment is down 77K since May 2025
), Consumer Discretionary (consumption slowed in Q1 under elevated gasoline/energy prices), and REITs (30y mortgage rate at 6.30%, cap rates pressure). Rate-sensitive small caps are also under pressure from the higher-for-longer repricing.

---

### 3. 👁️ WHAT TO WATCH — Top 2–3 Catalysts (Next 1–6 Months)

1. **Kevin Warsh Fed Chair Confirmation (~May 15):** 
Warsh's Senate confirmation appointment is set for May 15
 — his arrival could signal a more hawkish institutional tone and shift the Fed's reaction function. Any statement suggesting tolerance for higher inflation targets *or* faster rate hikes would be the single largest near-term repricing event for equities and long bonds.

2. **Iran/Strait of Hormuz Resolution or Escalation:** 
Long-term yields eased as oil and product prices whipsawed from their peaks, despite the impasse between Iran and the US that prolonged the suspension of energy exports from the key region.
 A deal that reopens exports would immediately knock 10–15% off crude, relief-rally the long end, and rotate capital from Energy/Defense back into rate-sensitives. A military escalation has the opposite effect — stagflation premium spikes.

3. **April CPI (due mid-May) & April Nonfarm Payrolls (May 2):** 
The prices paid component of ISM Manufacturing surged to a four-year high
, pre-signaling upside CPI risk. If April CPI prints ≥0.4% MoM core alongside a payrolls beat (above 150K), the remaining market pricing for any 2026 cut will evaporate entirely — and the 10y could test 4.60–4.75%, a genuine headwind for a market trading at 24x forward earnings with a negative equity risk premium.

---
*All data as of Friday May 1 / weekend of May 2–4, 2026. Sources: Federal Reserve, BLS, US Treasury, IMF Article IV, Trading Economics, Advisor Perspectives, GuruFocus, CNBC.*
